course.title: Kazakh, Start to Fluent
course.subtitle: Modern Kazakh from absolute beginner through fluent, everyday command — real sentences, grammar, and conversational practice
course.heroLedeSuffix: Real sentences from lesson one, Latin transliteration to guide pronunciation while you learn the Cyrillic script.

a1-pronouns-tobe.title: Personal Pronouns & "To Be"
a1-pronouns-tobe.explanation: Kazakh has no separate verb 'to be' in the present tense — instead, personal predicative endings attach directly to the noun or adjective, agreeing with the subject's person. E.g. мен студентпін (I am a student), сен студентсің (you are a student), ол студент (he/she is a student — no ending for 3rd person), біз студентпіз (we are students). The ending's exact shape depends on vowel harmony and whether the word ends in a vowel or a voiced/voiceless consonant.
a1-pronouns-tobe.fact: Because the 3rd person takes no ending at all, a bare sentence like "Ол дәрігер" already means the complete "He/she is a doctor" — there's no missing word to fill in, word order alone carries the meaning.

dialogue.en: I am a teacher. And you?

a1-possessive.title: Possessive Suffixes
a1-possessive.explanation: Kazakh marks possession by attaching a suffix directly to the possessed noun, not just with a separate possessive pronoun. Consonant-ending nouns take -ым/-ім (мен), -ың/-ің (сен), -ы/-і (ол); vowel-ending nouns take -м/-ң/-сы/-сі instead.
a1-possessive.fact: You'll hear the possessive suffix used alone, without the pronoun, all the time — "Атым Айгүл" ("My name is Aigul") is just as natural as "Менің атым Айгүл," because the suffix -ым already tells you it's 'my' name.

a1-plural.title: The Plural Suffix -лар/-лер
a1-plural.explanation: Kazakh plurals use one suffix family with six shapes, chosen by two rules together: vowel harmony (back-vowel word → -лар/-дар/-тар, front-vowel word → -лер/-дер/-тер) and the stem's final sound (vowel or voiced consonant → л-form, sonorant → д-form, voiceless consonant → т-form). E.g. бала → балалар (children), кітап → кітаптар (books), дос → достар (friends).
a1-plural.fact: After a number, Kazakh nouns usually drop the plural suffix entirely — "бес кітап" (five books), not "бес кітаптар" — the number already makes the plurality obvious, so adding -лар would sound redundant to a native speaker.

dialogue.en: How many books are there here?

a1-questions.title: Yes/No Questions: ма / ме / ба / бе / па / пе
a1-questions.explanation: Kazakh turns a statement into a yes/no question by adding a small particle right after the word being questioned — its shape (six variants) follows the same vowel-harmony-plus-voicing rule as plurals and possessives. Any personal ending (like -сың, -сіз) attaches after the particle, not before it.
a1-questions.fact: Because the particle attaches directly after the questioned word, Kazakh can target just one part of a sentence very precisely — "Сен дәрігерсің бе?" specifically asks whether it's a DOCTOR that you are, not a generic yes/no about the whole sentence.

a1-cases-1.title: Genitive & Dative Cases
a1-cases-1.explanation: The genitive marks a possessor noun (like English 's or 'of') and always pairs with a possessive suffix on the thing owned — "менің кітабым" literally "of-me my-book." The dative marks a destination, recipient, or purpose — "мектепке барамын" (I'm going TO school), "саған сыйлық" (a gift FOR you).
a1-cases-1.fact: The dative case is also how Kazakh expresses "I like/need/want": the person goes in dative plus a special verb, e.g. "Маған ұнайды" — literally "to-me it's-pleasing" — meaning "I like it."

a1-cases-2.title: Accusative, Locative & Ablative Cases
a1-cases-2.explanation: The accusative marks a definite direct object — "кітапты оқимын" (I'm reading THE book, a specific one) — versus a bare noun for something general or indefinite ("кітап оқимын", I'm reading [a] book). The locative marks a static location ("үйде" — at home). The ablative marks a starting point or source ("үйден" — from home).
a1-cases-2.fact: These three cases form a neat trio with the dative you already learned: dative -ға (TO), locative -да (AT), ablative -дан (FROM) — a single root like үй gives you үйге/үйде/үйден for "to/at/from home."

dialogue.en: Yes, I read the book at home.

a1-present.title: Present Tense: -а/-е/-й ды and -ып жатыр
a1-present.explanation: Kazakh has two present tenses. The general present (-а/-е/-йды) describes habits, facts, and near-future plans: "мен кітап оқимын" (I read / I'm going to read). The present continuous (-ып жатыр) describes an action happening right now: "мен кітап оқып жатырмын" (I am reading [right now]).
a1-present.fact: The continuous marker "жатыр" literally means "is lying down" — it grammaticalized from "lying there doing X" into a general "in the middle of doing X," which is why it's also used for actions with no literal lying-down, like "жаңбыр жауып жатыр" (it's raining).

dialogue.en: What are you doing right now?

a1-past.title: Definite Past Tense: -ды/-ді/-ты/-ті
a1-past.explanation: The definite past describes a completed action the speaker witnessed or is certain of. The suffix has four shapes chosen by the stem's final sound (vowel-harmony pair × voicing), and personal endings (-м, -ң, -қ/-к, none for 3rd person) attach right after it: келдім (I came), келдің (you came), келді (he/she came).
a1-past.fact: Kazakh also has a separate 'unwitnessed'/reported past (-ған/-ген) for things you heard about rather than saw yourself — mixing the two up is a classic learner slip, a bit like confusing "I did" with "apparently I did" in English.

dialogue.en: Yesterday I went to the movies.

a1-negation.title: Negation: жоқ, емес, and -ма/-ме
a1-negation.pattern: жоқ = 'there isn't/doesn't exist'; емес = 'is not' (after noun/adj); -ма/-ме/-ба/-бе/-па/-пе = verb negation suffix
a1-negation.explanation: Kazakh splits negation across three tools depending on what's negated. жоқ negates existence/possession: "Кітап жоқ" (There's no book / I don't have a book). емес negates an identity or description after a noun or adjective: "Бұл кітап емес" (This is not a book). A verb negates itself with -ма/-ме (etc.) inserted right before the tense ending: "келмедім" (I didn't come), "оқымаймын" (I don't read).
a1-negation.fact: жоқ doubles as the simple word for "no" (as иә is "yes"), so жоқ alone can be a complete one-word answer — unlike емес or the verb suffix, which always attach to something else.

a1-imperative.title: Giving Commands: The Imperative
a1-imperative.explanation: The plain command for someone you're close to or younger than you is just the bare verb stem, no ending: Кел! (Come!), Отыр! (Sit!). For anyone you'd address formally — an elder, a stranger, a boss — add -ыңыз/-іңіз: Келіңіз! (Please come!), Отырыңыз! (Please sit!).
a1-imperative.fact: The polite imperative suffix -ыңыз/-іңіз is the exact same suffix used for formal 'you' (сіз) agreement everywhere else in the grammar — Kazakh politeness marking is consistent across the whole system, not a one-off exception.

a1-comparison.title: Comparing Things: -рақ/-рек
a1-comparison.explanation: Kazakh makes a soft "somewhat more X" comparative by adding -рақ/-рек (or the buffer-vowel forms -ырақ/-ірек after a consonant) directly to the adjective: үлкен → үлкенірек (a bit bigger). To compare two things directly ("bigger THAN"), mark the thing compared against with the ablative case you already learned: "Бұл анадан үлкен" (This is bigger than that).
a1-comparison.fact: This comparative is deliberately soft and approximate — "a bit more, -ish" — rather than a strong "much more." For real emphasis, Kazakh stacks an intensifying word like "әлдеқайда" (far/much) in front instead of piling on more suffixes.

dialogue.en: This one is a bit bigger than that one.

a2-future.title: Future Tense
a2-future.explanation: Kazakh has two future forms. The intentional future (-мақ/-мек + a personal ending) expresses a plan: "бармақпын" (I intend to go). The simple/probable future (-ар/-ер, or bare -р after a vowel) is more general and slightly less certain: "барармын" (I will/probably will go).
a2-future.fact: The simple future's negative is irregular in a distinctive way — instead of the usual -ма- infix, it uses -мас: "бармаспын" (I probably won't go), one of the few places Kazakh negation breaks its own regular pattern.

a2-conditional.title: Conditional Mood
a2-conditional.explanation: The conditional suffix -са/-се attaches directly to the verb stem to mean "if [subject] does/did X" — барсам (if I go), келсең (if you come). It's usually followed by a clause stating the result, and can combine with "еді" for a hypothetical, counterfactual "if only" sense.
a2-conditional.fact: Kazakh doesn't need a separate word for "if" at all — the whole conditional meaning is packed into this one suffix on the verb, so "Барсам, көрермін" reads word-for-word as roughly "go-if, see-probably-I" = "If I go, I'll probably see [it]."

dialogue.en: If you had money, what would you buy?
If I had money, I would buy a house.

a2-converbs.title: Linking Clauses: Converbs
a2-converbs.explanation: Kazakh chains actions together with converbs instead of a word for "and" between clauses: "тұрып, кетті" (having stood up, [he] left = he stood up and left). Only the LAST verb in the chain carries the real tense/person ending — every earlier verb just takes the converb suffix.
a2-converbs.fact: This is why Kazakh sentences can chain many actions in a row with only one fully-conjugated verb at the very end — a whole sequence of events reads almost like a list of converbs building up to a single final, tensed verb.

dialogue.en: What do you do in the morning?
I get up, wash up, and eat.

a2-participles.title: Participles as Adjectives
a2-participles.explanation: Kazakh turns a whole verb clause into an adjective describing a noun using participles — "оқитын бала" literally "reading child" = "the child who reads/studies." The -атын/-етін form is habitual/general, while -ған/-ген describes a completed past action: "келген адам" (the person who came).
a2-participles.fact: This is how Kazakh builds relative clauses ("the book that I read") with no separate relative pronoun like English "that/which" — the participle suffix on the verb does that whole job by itself, right inside the noun phrase.

dialogue.en: I read the book that you gave me.

a2-necessitative.title: Expressing Necessity
a2-necessitative.explanation: Kazakh expresses obligation with the infinitive (-у) form of a verb followed by one of three necessity words: керек (need to, most common/neutral), тиіс (should, moral obligation), қажет (necessary, more formal).
a2-necessitative.fact: керек doubles as the general word for "need" outside verbs too — you already know it meaning "need" for objects ("Маған су керек" = I need water); attached after a verb's -у form, it just extends that same "needed" sense to actions.

dialogue.en: What do I need to do?
You need to go to the doctor.

a2-reported-speech.title: Reported Speech
a2-reported-speech.explanation: Kazakh reports what someone said with деп (literally "saying") placed right after the quoted words, followed by a verb like айтты (said) or ойлайды (thinks): "Мен барамын деп айтты" (He/she said, "I will go"). The hearsay past (-ыпты/депті) marks something the speaker only heard about, not witnessed firsthand.
a2-reported-speech.fact: деп does double duty as both a quotation marker and a general way to report thought or naming — the same construction covers "he said that...", "she thinks that...", and even "a place called..."

a2-instrumental.title: Instrumental Case
a2-instrumental.explanation: The instrumental case marks the tool, means, or accompanying person of an action — "қаламмен жаздым" (I wrote WITH a pen), "досыммен бардым" (I went WITH my friend). Its three forms follow the same vowel/consonant-voicing rule as most other suffixes you've learned.
a2-instrumental.fact: This same suffix also covers English "by [means of transport]" — "автобуспен бардым" (I went BY bus) uses the identical -мен, so "with a pen", "with a friend", and "by bus" are all grammatically the same construction.

dialogue.en: How (by what) do you get to school?

a2-superlative.title: Superlative: The Most ~
a2-superlative.explanation: Kazakh's superlative is the simplest comparison degree — just place the intensifier ең directly before the adjective: ең үлкен (the biggest), ең жақсы (the best). Unlike the comparative -рақ suffix, the superlative never changes the adjective's own form.
a2-superlative.fact: ең can stack with the comparative you already know for extra emphasis in casual speech — "ең үлкенірек" ("the most bigger-ish") sounds a bit redundant to a purist, similar to English "most biggest", but you'll still hear it.

dialogue.en: What is the biggest city in Kazakhstan?

a2-modality.title: Possibility & Permission
a2-modality.pattern: [stem]+-у + бола ма? / болады = "may I / is it allowed"; мүмкін = "maybe"
a2-modality.explanation: Asking permission uses the infinitive plus "бола ма?" (literally "is it allowed to be?"): "Кіруге бола ма?" (May I come in?), answered with "болады" (that's fine) or "болмайды" (not allowed). The separate word мүмкін means "maybe/possibly" and stands alone at the start of a sentence.
a2-modality.fact: болады/болмайды are also just the everyday words for "okay/not okay" in casual conversation — you'll hear them answering almost any yes/no request, not only formal permission questions.

dialogue.en: Yes, that's fine, please come in.

a2-passive.title: Passive Voice
a2-passive.explanation: The passive suffix -ыл/-іл (after a consonant) or -л (after a vowel) shifts focus from who did an action to what happened to it: "кітап жазылды" (the book was written), instead of naming who wrote it.
a2-passive.fact: Passive constructions are common in Kazakh news and official writing specifically to avoid naming an actor — "шешім қабылданды" (a decision was made/adopted) sounds appropriately formal and impersonal, the same rhetorical move as English bureaucratic passive voice.

dialogue.en: This house was built this year.

a2-causative.title: Causative Voice: Make/Let Someone Do
a2-causative.explanation: The causative suffix turns "X does Y" into "someone makes/lets X do Y" — оқыды (he studied) becomes оқытты (he taught, literally "made [someone] study"). Which shape attaches depends on the verb stem's last sound, the same families of rules you've seen elsewhere.
a2-causative.fact: This is exactly how Kazakh derives its native verb for "to teach" — оқыту is built from оқу ("to study") purely with this causative suffix, no separate root needed, unlike the borrowed noun мұғалім ("teacher").

a2-numbers-dates.title: Larger Numbers, Ordinals & Dates
a2-numbers-dates.pattern: Round numbers: отыз (30), жүз (100), мың (1000); Ordinals: [number]+-ыншы/-інші
a2-numbers-dates.explanation: Kazakh builds larger numbers by simple concatenation, like English "twenty-one": жиырма бір (21) is literally "twenty one." Round numbers have their own roots — отыз (30), қырық (40), елу (50), алпыс (60), жетпіс (70), сексен (80), тоқсан (90), жүз (100), мың (1000). Ordinals add -ыншы/-інші to the cardinal: бірінші (first), екінші (second).
a2-numbers-dates.fact: Years are just spoken as one big cardinal number followed by "жыл" (year) — 2024 is "екі мың жиырма төрт", built the exact same concatenative way as any other large number.

dialogue.en: Today is the first of September.

b1-perfect.title: Resultative/Perfect Aspect
b1-perfect.explanation: Kazakh marks a present-relevant completed action with the past participle plus бар (exists) or жоқ (doesn't exist): "көрген бар" (I have seen it, the experience exists) versus the simple past "көрдім" (I saw it, just narrating the event). This highlights that the RESULT still matters now.
b1-perfect.fact: This is genuinely close to English's present perfect ("I have seen") versus simple past ("I saw") distinction — a useful mental shortcut, even though the literal Kazakh grammar (participle + "exists/doesn't exist") works completely differently under the hood.

dialogue.en: Have you ever seen this movie?

b1-past-continuous.title: Past Continuous
b1-past-continuous.explanation: Add "еді" (the past-tense copula) after the present continuous (-ып жатыр) to shift the whole "in the middle of doing X" meaning into the past: "мен оқып жатыр едім" (I was reading, at that time).
b1-past-continuous.fact: еді is the same little "was" word that turns almost any present-tense construction into its past equivalent throughout Kazakh grammar — you'll meet it again with conditionals and other moods doing exactly this same job.

dialogue.native: Мен қоңырау шалғанда не істеп жатыр едің?
dialogue.en: What were you doing when I called?

b1-desiderative.title: Desire: "I Want To..."
b1-desiderative.explanation: Kazakh's "want to" construction attaches a possessive-flavored suffix to the verb stem (matching the person who wants) plus келеді (literally "comes/arises"): "барғым келеді" (I want to go, literally "my going arises").
b1-desiderative.fact: The suffix on the verb is technically a possessive form — "барғым" is built the same way as "my book" possessives you already learned — which is why the wanting person shows up as an ending ON THE VERB rather than as a subject agreeing with келеді itself.

dialogue.en: What do you want to do?

b1-past-obligation.title: Past Obligation: Should Have
b1-past-obligation.pattern: [stem]+-у + керек еді / тиіс еді = "should have / had to"
b1-past-obligation.explanation: Adding еді after керек or тиіс shifts obligation into the past, usually implying the thing wasn't actually done — much like English "should have": "баруым керек еді" ("I should have gone", but didn't).
b1-past-obligation.fact: This same еді-adds-hindsight pattern is why Kazakh speakers often layer regret onto a past-obligation sentence without needing any extra words — the missed obligation is baked right into the grammar.

dialogue.en: I should have gotten up early.

b1-verbal-nouns.title: Verbal Nouns
b1-verbal-nouns.pattern: [stem]+-у = a noun meaning "the act of doing X"
b1-verbal-nouns.explanation: The infinitive -у form doubles as a genuine noun that can be a sentence's subject or object, take case endings, and even take possessive suffixes: "оқу қызық" (studying/reading is interesting), "жүзуді жақсы көремін" (I like swimming — жүзу+ді, accusative).
b1-verbal-nouns.fact: This is the same -у form you already used inside "need to" (-у керек) and "may I" (-у бола ма) — not a separate grammar point, just that same infinitive-as-noun idea now standing as a full independent subject or object.

b1-concessive.title: Concession: "Even If / Although"
b1-concessive.explanation: Adding да after the conditional -са/-се flips its meaning from "if" to "even if/although": "жаңбыр жауса да, барамын" (Even if it rains, I'll go). The standalone word дегенмен ("however/nevertheless") links two contrasting full sentences instead.
b1-concessive.fact: This -са да pattern is a nice two-for-one — you already know -са means "if", so concession costs you just one extra syllable (да) rather than a whole new suffix family.

dialogue.en: Will you go even if it rains?
Yes, I'll go even if it rains.

b1-purpose.title: Purpose Clauses
b1-purpose.pattern: [noun/verbal noun] + үшін = "in order to / for the sake of"
b1-purpose.explanation: үшін (literally "for the sake of") attaches after a noun or a verbal noun (-у form) to express purpose: "сен үшін" (for you), "оқу үшін" (in order to study).
b1-purpose.fact: үшін marks purpose for a PERSON ("for you") and purpose for an ACTION ("in order to study") with the exact same word — English needs two different constructions ("for" vs "in order to") to cover what Kazakh does with one postposition.

dialogue.en: In order to talk with my family.

b1-equative.title: Equative: "Like/As ~ As"
b1-equative.explanation: The equative suffix compares something to a noun directly, no separate word for "like" needed: "арыстандай күшті" (strong as a lion), "балдай тәтті" (sweet as honey).
b1-equative.fact: This tight, one-suffix comparison is why Kazakh proverbs and everyday speech are packed with vivid, compact similes that would need a whole extra phrase ("as ... as a ...") in English.

dialogue.en: What kind of person is he/she?

b1-emphatic-particles.title: Emphatic Particles: ғой, тіпті, өзі
b1-emphatic-particles.explanation: Kazakh sprinkles small particles into sentences to add tone rather than new information. ғой softens a statement into a gentle reminder ("you know, right"): "Мен айттым ғой" (I did tell you, [remember]). тіпті means "even": "тіпті балалар біледі" (even children know). өзі emphasizes "itself/the very": "бүгін өзі" (today itself).
b1-emphatic-particles.fact: ғой is one of the most frequent words in casual spoken Kazakh, yet it doesn't translate to any single English word — dropping the right amount of ғой into a sentence is one of the clearest tells of natural, comfortable fluency versus stiff textbook speech.

dialogue.en: Do you know there's an exam tomorrow?

b1-negative-pronouns.title: Negative Pronouns
b1-negative-pronouns.explanation: Kazakh's "no-" pronoun family (еш- + kim/qaşan/bir/teñe) always needs a negated verb alongside it, unlike English which drops verb negation once it has a "no-" word: "ешкім келмеді" literally "nobody didn't-come" = "nobody came."
b1-negative-pronouns.fact: This double-marking (a negative pronoun PLUS a negated verb) is actually the cross-linguistic norm — Standard English is the unusual one for allowing only a single negation per clause; Kazakh's pattern matches Russian, Spanish, and many other languages instead.

b1plus-habitual-conditional.title: Habitual Conditional: "Whenever"
b1plus-habitual-conditional.explanation: The same -са/-се conditional suffix can express a HABITUAL pattern rather than a one-time hypothetical, when paired with a habitual-tense result clause: "Жаңбыр жауса, көше сулы болады" (Whenever it rains, the street gets wet) — a general truth, not a single hypothetical event.
b1plus-habitual-conditional.fact: Context alone tells you whether -са/-се means a one-off hypothetical ("if") or a repeated pattern ("whenever") — Kazakh doesn't mark this with a different suffix, so the habitual-tense verb in the result clause is your main clue.

dialogue.en: Whenever it's cold, what do you do? (informal)

b1plus-comparative-clauses.title: "The More... The More..."
b1plus-comparative-clauses.explanation: Kazakh builds a parallel-increase comparison with -ған сайын ("the more [X happens]"), often paired with another -ған сайын clause or a simple result: "Оқыған сайын білім артады" (The more you study, the more knowledge grows).
b1plus-comparative-clauses.fact: сайын alone (without -ған) means "every" (жыл сайын, every year) — the -ған сайын construction builds on that same "each time/every" sense, extended to mean "with each increment of X".

dialogue.en: Why do you study so much? (informal)
The more I study, the more my knowledge grows.

b1plus-reflexive.title: Reflexive Actions
b1plus-reflexive.pattern: өзін/өзіне/өзімен + verb = doing something to/for oneself
b1plus-reflexive.explanation: Kazakh marks a reflexive action with the reflexive pronoun өз ("self") taking the appropriate case ending: "өзін көрді" (saw himself/herself), "өзіне сатып алды" (bought for himself/herself).
b1plus-reflexive.fact: өз also means "own" as a possessive intensifier outside reflexive contexts ("өз үйім", my own house) — the same root covers both "self" and "own", a natural semantic overlap also seen in many other languages.

dialogue.en: What did he/she buy for himself/herself?

b1plus-reciprocal.title: Reciprocal Actions
b1plus-reciprocal.explanation: Adding -ыс/-іс to a verb stem turns it reciprocal — an action done mutually between two or more people: көру (to see) → көрісу (to see each other/greet), сөйлеу (to speak) → сөйлесу (to converse). One of the most productive suffixes for describing social interaction.
b1plus-reciprocal.fact: This is the exact suffix family behind сөйлесу, the everyday word for "to converse" — its reciprocal meaning is baked in so thoroughly that most speakers don't consciously think of it as a special suffix at all.

dialogue.en: Do you two see each other often? (informal)

b1plus-degree-adverbs.title: Degree Adverbs
b1plus-degree-adverbs.explanation: тым intensifies negatively ("too much"): тым қымбат (too expensive). әбден means "thoroughly/quite": әбден шаршадым (I'm thoroughly tired). мүлдем means "entirely", often in negative sentences: мүлдем білмеймін (I don't know at all).
b1plus-degree-adverbs.fact: мүлдем in a negative sentence works like English "not... at all" — it makes the denial total and absolute, useful when you want to firmly rule something out.

b1plus-formal-conditional.title: Formal Conditional Connector
b1plus-formal-conditional.explanation: While -са/-се alone can mean "if", adding егер at the start of the clause makes the conditional more explicit and formal, common in written or careful speech: "Егер уақытың болса, келші" (If you have time, please come).
b1plus-formal-conditional.fact: егер itself doesn't carry grammatical meaning on its own — it's purely there to flag "a conditional is coming" up front, similar to how English "if" announces itself before the rest of the clause unfolds.

dialogue.en: If it rains, what will we do?

b1plus-time-duration.title: Duration Expressions
b1plus-time-duration.explanation: бойы marks a stretch of time something continued throughout: күні бойы (all day long). ішінде marks a deadline/window: бір апта ішінде (within a week). аралығында marks a range: екі мен үш арасында (between two and three).
b1plus-time-duration.fact: бойы is related to the noun "бой" (body/length/extent) — a continuous physical length metaphorically extended to a continuous stretch of TIME, a common metaphor across languages.

dialogue.en: What did you do all day long? (informal)

b1plus-consequence.title: Consequence Clauses
b1plus-consequence.pattern: нәтижесінде (as a result), салдарынан (because of/as a consequence)
b1plus-consequence.explanation: нәтижесінде frames a positive/neutral outcome: "Тырысудың нәтижесінде жетістікке жетті" (As a result of the effort, he/she achieved success). салдарынан often frames a negative consequence: "Немқұрайлылықтың салдарынан қате кетті" (As a consequence of carelessness, a mistake happened).
b1plus-consequence.fact: The choice between them is a subtle register/tone cue — swapping them can make a neutral outcome sound needlessly negative, or a genuine problem sound suspiciously neutral.

dialogue.en: As a consequence of insufficient preparation.

b1plus-approximation.title: Approximation & Estimation
b1plus-approximation.explanation: шамамен hedges any number: шамамен он адам (approximately ten people). төңірегінде also means "around" for numbers, slightly more formal. жуық means "nearly/close to": мыңға жуық (close to a thousand).
b1plus-approximation.fact: төңірегінде literally means "in the vicinity/surroundings of" (its concrete sense is spatial) — extending a spatial "around" word to numeric approximation is the same metaphor English uses with "around ten people".

b1plus-reported-commands.title: Reported Commands & Requests
b1plus-reported-commands.explanation: To report someone else's command, Kazakh keeps the imperative form of the original words and wraps it with деп plus a reporting verb: "Кел деп айтты" (He/she told me to come, literally "'Come!' saying, said"). The same деп pattern as reported speech, applied to commands.
b1plus-reported-commands.fact: Because the ORIGINAL imperative word stays unchanged inside the деп wrapper, reporting a command in Kazakh is often more literal/direct-sounding than English's shift into infinitives ("told me to come") — you're quoting the command, not converting its grammatical form.

dialogue.en: What did he/she say to you? (informal)
He/she told me to come early.

b2-wishes.title: Wishes & Regrets: "If Only"
b2-wishes.explanation: Kazakh expresses a wistful wish or regret by combining the conditional (-са) with the particle екен: "Барса екен!" (If only he/she would go!), or for past regret, "Барған болса екен" (If only he/she had gone). The екен adds a note of hope or realization, distinct from a plain conditional statement.
b2-wishes.fact: You'll often hear "екен" tacked onto an ordinary sentence to mark that the speaker just realized or is reacting to something — "Жаңбыр жауып тұр екен!" ("Oh, it's raining!", as if noticing for the first time) — the same particle that flavors wishes also flavors fresh realizations.

b2-indirect-questions.title: Indirect Questions
b2-indirect-questions.pattern: [embedded question] + ма/ме + екенін білу/сұрау = "to know/ask whether..."
b2-indirect-questions.explanation: Kazakh embeds a yes/no question inside a larger sentence by keeping the original question particle and adding -екенін ("that it is") before the main verb: "Ол келе ме екенін білмеймін" (I don't know whether he/she is coming). For a wh-question, the question word stays, still followed by екенін.
b2-indirect-questions.fact: This -екен(ін) suffix does the job English does with "whether/if" or "that" clauses — but it rides on the END of the embedded question rather than introducing it, mirroring Kazakh's general head-final word order.

dialogue.native: Ол келе ме екенін білесің бе?
dialogue.en: Do you know whether he/she is coming? (informal)

b2-double-cases.title: Stacking Suffixes
b2-double-cases.explanation: Kazakh nouns can stack several suffixes in a fixed order to build compact phrases English needs several words for: genitive marks a possessor, a possessive suffix marks the possessed thing, and a further case suffix can attach on top: "мектептің ауласындағы бала" ("the child in the schoolyard", literally "school-of yard-its-in child").
b2-double-cases.fact: The suffix order is fixed and never scrambles — once you know it, you can build arbitrarily long, precise noun phrases just by chaining suffixes onto a single root.

b2-aspectual-verbs.title: Aspectual Auxiliary Verbs
b2-aspectual-verbs.pattern: [converb -ып/-іп] + тастау/қалу/шығу/жіберу = nuance of how an action completed
b2-aspectual-verbs.explanation: Kazakh adds nuance to a verb's completion by pairing its converb form with a light auxiliary: "жазып тастады" (finished writing decisively), "ұмытып қалды" (ended up forgetting), "естіп қалды" (happened to hear). Each auxiliary carries its own flavor of completion.
b2-aspectual-verbs.fact: These auxiliaries have mostly bleached out their original literal meaning (тастау alone means "to throw/abandon", қалу means "to stay/remain") — here they're pure grammatical nuance markers, similar to how English "end up doing" doesn't literally involve an end.

dialogue.en: Did you finish writing the letter? (informal)

b2-modal-nuance.title: Degrees of Certainty
b2-modal-nuance.explanation: шығар (from the verb "to come out") softens a guess: "Үйде шығар" (He's probably home). болар is similar but leans toward future likelihood: "Жақсы болар" (It'll probably be fine). мүмкін stands alone at the sentence start for a more open "maybe".
b2-modal-nuance.fact: шығар and болар both grammaticalized from ordinary verbs ("to emerge", "to become/be") into fixed hedging particles — a common path across languages where a verb meaning "to seem/emerge" turns into an evidentiality marker over time.

b2-nominalization.title: Formal Nominalization
b2-nominalization.explanation: Beyond the simple -у infinitive-noun, Kazakh has more formal nominalizing patterns common in official and written registers: "келісу" (to agree) relates to "келісім" (an agreement), "шешу" (to decide) to "шешім" (a decision). These nominal forms appear constantly in official documents, reports, and news.
b2-nominalization.fact: Formal Kazakh writing leans heavily on nominalizations instead of full verb clauses, much like English officialese prefers "the implementation of" over "we implemented" — recognizing this pattern helps you read news and official texts more fluently.

b2-discourse-connectors.title: Advanced Connectors
b2-discourse-connectors.explanation: These connectors link whole sentences in formal or careful speech, similar to English "therefore", "because", "otherwise", "nevertheless". сондықтан and өйткені are common in everyday explanation; әйтпесе and соған қарамастан show up more in argumentative or written contexts.
b2-discourse-connectors.fact: өйткені almost always starts a NEW sentence explaining the previous one, rather than joining mid-sentence like English "because" often does — Kazakh tends to state the result first, then a separate өйткені-sentence for the reason.

dialogue.en: The bus didn't come, so I was late.

b2-relative-time.title: Relative Time Clauses
b2-relative-time.explanation: To sequence two actions precisely, Kazakh attaches -мастан бұрын ("before doing X") or -ғаннан кейін ("after doing X") to the first verb: "тамақ ішместен бұрын қолымды жудым" (I washed my hands before eating), "жұмыстан келгеннен кейін демалдым" (I rested after coming from work).
b2-relative-time.fact: -мастан бұрын is built from the negative converb (-мас, "not yet") plus бұрын ("before") — literally "before not-yet-doing", a logical way to anchor "before X" to the moment X hasn't happened yet.

dialogue.en: What do you do before eating? (informal)

b2-hedging.title: Softening Statements
b2-hedging.explanation: сияқты softens a claim into an impression: "Ол шаршаған сияқты" (He/she seems tired). шамамен hedges a number: "шамамен бес сағат" (approximately five hours). әлде stands between two options to signal uncertainty: "Ол келеді, әлде келмейді" (He'll come, or maybe not).
b2-hedging.fact: сияқты is literally built from the equative -дай/-дей family you already know plus a support noun — the same underlying comparison logic you first met with "арыстандай батыл" (brave as a lion).

b2-passive-causative-combo.title: Combining Passive & Causative
b2-passive-causative-combo.explanation: The causative and passive suffixes can stack on the same verb — causative first, then passive — to express that someone was made to have something done, or that an action was caused and then happened impersonally: "оқытылды" (was taught, from оқу→оқыт→оқытыл), "тазартылды" (was made clean).
b2-passive-causative-combo.fact: This stacking order — causative before passive — is fixed and mirrors the logical order of events: first someone causes the action, then the passive removes the causer from focus, leaving only the impersonal "was done" result.

b2plus-formal-recommendation.title: Formal Recommendation
b2plus-formal-recommendation.explanation: Beyond everyday керек/тиіс, formal and written Kazakh often recommends an action with a participle plus жөн ("proper/advisable") or дұрыс ("correct"): "Барғаны жөн" (It would be advisable to go), literally "the having-gone is proper". Reads as more measured and formal than a direct керек command.
b2plus-formal-recommendation.fact: This pattern turns the ACTION itself into the grammatical subject of the sentence (via the participle), which is why English needs a whole "it would be advisable to..." framing to capture the same indirect, impersonal tone Kazakh gets from a single participle plus жөн.

dialogue.en: What would be advisable to do?
It would be right to see a doctor.

b2plus-participial-clauses.title: Complex Participial Clauses
b2plus-participial-clauses.explanation: Advanced Kazakh routinely stacks several participle clauses before a single noun to pack in description without any relative pronoun: "кеше көшеде көрген ... адам" ("the person [I] saw on the street yesterday"), each descriptive clause landing directly before the noun it modifies.
b2plus-participial-clauses.fact: Because everything modifying the noun must come BEFORE it, a complex Kazakh sentence can front-load a long chain of clauses before you reach the noun itself — a different reading rhythm from English, where relative clauses usually trail after the noun.

dialogue.native: Кеше көшеде көрген адамды білесің бе?
dialogue.en: Do you know the person I saw on the street yesterday? (informal)

b2plus-emphasis-inversion.title: Emphatic Word Order
b2plus-emphasis-inversion.pattern: fronting the emphasized element, or repeating it for stress
b2plus-emphasis-inversion.explanation: Kazakh's normally flexible word order can shift for emphasis: moving a word to the very front or right before the verb highlights it as new or contrastive information. Repetition also marks emphasis: "Жақсы, жақсы" (Fine, fine) or "Тез, тез!" (Quick, quick!).
b2plus-emphasis-inversion.fact: Because Kazakh marks grammatical roles with case suffixes rather than strict word order, the language can freely reorder words for emphasis without ever becoming ambiguous about who did what to whom — a flexibility fixed-word-order languages like English don't have.

b2plus-evidentiality.title: Evidentiality Nuance
b2plus-evidentiality.explanation: Advanced Kazakh distinguishes HOW you know something with different past-tense forms: the plain definite past for something you witnessed directly, -ыпты/депті for hearsay, and екен for a fact you're realizing or inferring in the moment. Choosing the wrong one signals the wrong source of knowledge.
b2plus-evidentiality.fact: This three-way evidential distinction is a genuinely different grammatical category from English, which uses separate words ("I saw", "I heard", "apparently") rather than baking the distinction into the verb ending — getting it right is one of the last things advanced learners master.

dialogue.en: Apparently he/she came — I didn't see it myself, I heard about it.

b2plus-conditional-nuance.title: Mixed & Counterfactual Conditionals
b2plus-conditional-nuance.pattern: past conditional + еді on both clauses for full counterfactuals
b2plus-conditional-nuance.explanation: A full counterfactual combines the conditional with еді on BOTH clauses: "Уақытым болса, барар едім" (If I had time, I would go) versus the stronger past regret "Уақытым болған болса, барар едім" (If I had had time, I would have gone). Mixing past condition with present result lets you express nuanced "if things had been different" logic.
b2plus-conditional-nuance.fact: This mirrors English's second/third conditional distinction ("if I had time, I would go" vs "if I had had time, I would have gone") almost suffix-for-suffix — one of the rare grammar points where Kazakh and English map onto each other quite closely.

dialogue.en: If I had had time, I would have gone.

b2plus-formal-register.title: Formal/Bureaucratic Register
b2plus-formal-register.pattern: passive-heavy phrasing, formal connectors (алайда, осыған орай)
b2plus-formal-register.explanation: Official Kazakh (laws, announcements, formal letters) favors passive constructions, nominalized verbs, and formal connectors like алайда ("however", more formal than бірақ) and осыған орай ("in this regard"). Sentences tend to be longer and more impersonal than everyday speech.
b2plus-formal-register.fact: The Kazakh you hear in casual conversation and the Kazakh printed on official documents can feel like two different registers of the same language, similar to the gap between spoken and legal/bureaucratic English.

dialogue.en: Yes, it's written in very formal language.

b2plus-idioms.title: Idioms & Set Phrases
b2plus-idioms.explanation: Like any mature language, Kazakh has a rich stock of idioms whose meaning isn't literal: "көзін ашып-жұмғанша" (in the blink of an eye), "тілі байланды" (tongue-tied), "жүрегі жарылардай" (heart about to burst, i.e. extremely anxious/moved).
b2plus-idioms.fact: Idioms are one of the clearest markers of true fluency — using "көзін ашып-жұмғанша" naturally in conversation signals a completely different level of comfort with the language than always reaching for the literal "тез арада" (quickly).

dialogue.en: Did you finish the work quickly? (informal)
Yes, it was done in the blink of an eye.

b2plus-rhetorical-devices.title: Rhetorical Questions & Repetition
b2plus-rhetorical-devices.pattern: rhetorical question form; deliberate repetition for effect
b2plus-rhetorical-devices.explanation: Persuasive and literary Kazakh uses rhetorical questions that expect no answer, just emphasis: "Бұл әділетті ме?" (Is this fair? — implying clearly not) or "Кім білмейді?" (Who doesn't know? — implying everyone knows). Deliberate repetition also builds rhetorical force, common in speeches.
b2plus-rhetorical-devices.fact: Recognizing a rhetorical question as rhetorical (not a genuine request for information) is essential for correctly reading tone in formal or persuasive Kazakh texts and speeches.

b2plus-compound-sentences.title: Complex Multi-Clause Sentences
b2plus-compound-sentences.pattern: chaining converbs + subordinate clauses + one final main clause
b2plus-compound-sentences.explanation: Truly advanced Kazakh sentences chain multiple converbs, participial clauses, and subordinate clauses before arriving at one final, fully-conjugated main verb — combining conditionals, causes, and purposes all in a single long sentence.
b2plus-compound-sentences.fact: Reading long-form Kazakh journalism or literature well means holding several suspended clauses in mind until that one final verb resolves the whole sentence — much like parsing a long, subordinate-clause-heavy sentence in formal English writing.

dialogue.native: Көлік кешігіп, жол жабылып қалғандықтан, кешіктім.
dialogue.en: Because the bus was late and the road got closed, I was late.

b2plus-literary-style.title: Literary & Poetic Register
b2plus-literary-style.pattern: elevated vocabulary (арман, қиял, сағыныш), inverted syntax for effect
b2plus-literary-style.explanation: Kazakh literature and poetry draw on an elevated register with older vocabulary and rich imagery — words like арман (cherished dream/aspiration), қиял (imagination/fantasy), and сағыныш (longing/nostalgia) appear far more in literary contexts than casual speech.
b2plus-literary-style.fact: Kazakh has a strong oral literary tradition (akyns — improvising poet-singers) that still shapes how elevated, poetic language sounds today — even modern song lyrics and speeches borrow this register's rhythm and vocabulary.

dialogue.en: Traveling the world is my dream.

lessons.title: A Day in Aigul's Life
lessons.description: Айгүлдің әдеттегі бір күні туралы қысқа әңгіме.

readingPassage.context: Айгүлдің әдеттегі бір күні туралы қысқа әңгіме.

paragraphs.native: Айгүл — жиырма жасар студент.
paragraphs.en: Aigul is a twenty-year-old student.

paragraphs.native: Ол таңертең сағат жетіде тұрады.
paragraphs.en: She gets up at seven in the morning.

paragraphs.native: Таңғы ас ішіп, мектепке барады.
paragraphs.en: She eats breakfast and goes to school.

paragraphs.native: Мектепте ол ағылшын тілін үйренеді.
paragraphs.en: At school she studies English.

paragraphs.native: Түстен кейін достарымен кездеседі.
paragraphs.en: In the afternoon she meets with her friends.

paragraphs.native: Кешке үйге қайтып, тамақ пісіреді.
paragraphs.en: In the evening she returns home and cooks food.

paragraphs.native: Түнде кітап оқып, ұйықтайды.
paragraphs.en: At night she reads a book and sleeps.

exercises.question: How old is Aigul?
What does Aigul study at school?
What does she do in the evening?

readingPassage.context: Ерланның отбасы туралы қысқа әңгіме.

paragraphs.native: Ерланның үлкен отбасы бар.
paragraphs.en: Erlan has a big family.

paragraphs.native: Оның әкесі — дәрігер, анасы — мұғалім.
paragraphs.en: His father is a doctor, his mother is a teacher.

paragraphs.native: Ерланның екі бауыры бар: бір ағасы, бір қарындасы.
paragraphs.en: Erlan has two siblings: one older brother, one younger sister.

paragraphs.native: Олар Алматыда, үлкен үйде тұрады.
paragraphs.en: They live in Almaty, in a big house.

paragraphs.native: Демалыс күндері бәрі бірге тамақ ішеді.
paragraphs.en: On weekends everyone eats together.

paragraphs.native: Ерлан отбасын өте жақсы көреді.
paragraphs.en: Erlan loves his family very much.

exercises.question: What is Erlan's father's profession?
Where does Erlan's family live?
How many siblings does Erlan have?

lessons.description: Мадинаның дүкенге барғаны туралы қысқа әңгіме.

readingPassage.context: Мадинаның дүкенге барғаны туралы қысқа әңгіме.

paragraphs.native: Мадина дүкенге барды.
paragraphs.en: Madina went to the shop.

paragraphs.native: Ол нан, сүт және алма сатып алды.
paragraphs.en: She bought bread, milk, and apples.

paragraphs.native: Алма қымбат болды, бірақ нан арзан болды.
paragraphs.en: The apples were expensive, but the bread was cheap.

paragraphs.native: Сатушы оған жеңілдік берді.
paragraphs.en: The salesperson gave her a discount.

paragraphs.native: Мадина ақшасын төледі және үйге кетті.
paragraphs.en: Madina paid her money and went home.

lessons.title: A Trip to Turkistan
lessons.description: Данияр деген жігіттің Түркістанға саяхаты туралы қысқа әңгіме.

readingPassage.context: Данияр деген жігіттің Түркістанға саяхаты туралы қысқа әңгіме.

paragraphs.native: Жаз бойы Данияр Түркістанға саяхаттауды жоспарлады.
paragraphs.en: Over the summer, Daniyar planned to travel to Turkistan.

paragraphs.native: Ол пойызбен барды, себебі жол ұзақ болды.
paragraphs.en: He went by train, because the journey was long.

paragraphs.native: Түркістанда ол Қожа Ахмет Ясауи кесенесін көрді.
paragraphs.en: In Turkistan, he saw the Khoja Ahmet Yasawi Mausoleum.

paragraphs.native: Кесене өте әдемі және тарихи маңызды.
paragraphs.en: The mausoleum is very beautiful and historically significant.

paragraphs.native: Данияр жергілікті тағамдарды дәмдеп көрді.
paragraphs.en: Daniyar tried the local food.

paragraphs.native: Ол саяхаттан көп әсер алды.
paragraphs.en: He got a lot of impressions from the trip.

exercises.question: How did Daniyar travel to Turkistan?
What did he see in Turkistan?
What did Daniyar do with local food?

readingPassage.context: Наурыз мерекесі туралы қысқа мәтін.

paragraphs.native: Наурыз — көктемгі мереке.
paragraphs.en: Nauryz is a spring holiday.

paragraphs.native: Ол әрбір жылдың 22 наурызында тойланады.
paragraphs.en: It is celebrated every year on March 22nd.

paragraphs.native: Бұл күні адамдар жаңа жылды қарсы алады.
paragraphs.en: On this day, people welcome the new year.

paragraphs.native: Дастарханда наурыз көже дайындалады.
paragraphs.en: Nauryz kozhe is prepared on the table.

paragraphs.native: Отбасылар бірге жиналып, той өткізеді.
paragraphs.en: Families gather together and hold a celebration.

paragraphs.native: Балалар ән айтып, би билейді.
paragraphs.en: Children sing songs and dance.

exercises.question: When is Nauryz celebrated?
What dish is prepared for Nauryz?
What do children do during Nauryz?

lessons.title: A Job Interview Story
lessons.description: Айгерімнің жұмысқа сұхбатқа баруы туралы қысқа әңгіме.

readingPassage.context: Айгерімнің жұмысқа сұхбатқа баруы туралы қысқа әңгіме.

paragraphs.native: Айгерім жаңа жұмысқа сұхбатқа барды.
paragraphs.en: Aigerim went to an interview for a new job.

paragraphs.native: Ол ерте тұрып, жақсы киінді.
paragraphs.en: She got up early and dressed well.

paragraphs.native: Сұхбатта олар оның тәжірибесі туралы сұрады.
paragraphs.en: In the interview, they asked about her experience.

paragraphs.native: Айгерім сенімді түрде жауап берді.
paragraphs.en: Aigerim answered confidently.

paragraphs.native: Бір апта өткен соң, оған қоңырау шалды.
paragraphs.en: A week later, they called her.

paragraphs.native: Ол жұмысқа қабылданды!
paragraphs.en: She was hired!

exercises.question: What did Aigerim do before the interview?
What did they ask about?
What was the result?

lessons.title: Starting University
lessons.description: Бекзаттың университетке түсуі туралы қысқа әңгіме.

readingPassage.context: Бекзаттың университетке түсуі туралы қысқа әңгіме.

paragraphs.native: Бекзат биыл университетке түсті.
paragraphs.en: Bekzat entered university this year.

paragraphs.native: Ол инженерлік факультетте оқиды.
paragraphs.en: He studies at the engineering faculty.

paragraphs.native: Бірінші күні ол көп жаңа адаммен танысты.
paragraphs.en: On the first day, he met many new people.

lessons.title: Climate Change in Kazakhstan
lessons.description: Қазақстандағы климаттың өзгеруі туралы қысқа мәтін.

readingPassage.context: Қазақстандағы климаттың өзгеруі туралы қысқа мәтін.

paragraphs.native: Соңғы жылдары Қазақстанда ауа-райы өзгеріп жатыр.
paragraphs.en: In recent years, the weather in Kazakhstan has been changing.

paragraphs.native: Жаздар ыстығырақ, қыстар жылырақ болып барады.
paragraphs.en: Summers are getting hotter, winters are getting warmer.

paragraphs.native: Мұздықтар баяу еріп жатыр.
paragraphs.en: Glaciers are slowly melting.

paragraphs.native: Ғалымдар бұл климаттың өзгеруіне байланысты дейді.
paragraphs.en: Scientists say this is related to climate change.

paragraphs.native: Үкімет қоршаған ортаны қорғау шараларын қабылдады.
paragraphs.en: The government adopted measures to protect the environment.

paragraphs.native: Әр адам табиғатты сақтауға үлес қоса алады.
paragraphs.en: Every person can contribute to preserving nature.

exercises.question: What is happening to summers according to the passage?
What is slowly melting?
What did the government do?

lessons.title: A Local Business Success Story
lessons.description: Айдана есімді кәсіпкердің табысы туралы қысқа әңгіме.

readingPassage.context: Айдана есімді кәсіпкердің табысы туралы қысқа әңгіме.

paragraphs.native: Айдана бес жыл бұрын кішкентай кәсіп бастады.
paragraphs.en: Aidana started a small business five years ago.

paragraphs.native: Ол үй жағдайында тәтті пісіретін.
paragraphs.en: She used to bake sweets at home.

paragraphs.native: Алдымен достары мен көршілері сатып алатын.
paragraphs.en: At first, her friends and neighbors would buy them.

paragraphs.native: Кейін ол әлеуметтік желіде жарнамалай бастады.
paragraphs.en: Later she started advertising on social media.

paragraphs.native: Қазір оның кәсібі қалада танымал.
paragraphs.en: Now her business is famous in the city.

paragraphs.native: Айдана он адамға жұмыс береді.
paragraphs.en: Aidana employs ten people.

paragraphs.native: Ол басқаларға да кеңес беруге дайын.
paragraphs.en: She is ready to give advice to others too.

lessons.title: Traditions vs. Modern Life
lessons.description: Дәстүр мен заманауи өмір арасындағы қатынас туралы мәтін.

readingPassage.context: Дәстүр мен заманауи өмір арасындағы қатынас туралы мәтін.

paragraphs.native: Қазіргі жастар дәстүр мен заманауи өмір арасында.
paragraphs.en: Today's youth are between tradition and modern life.

paragraphs.native: Кейбіреулер ата-баба дәстүрін сақтауды маңызды деп санайды.
paragraphs.en: Some consider preserving ancestral traditions important.

paragraphs.native: Басқалары жаңа технологияға көбірек көңіл бөледі.
paragraphs.en: Others pay more attention to new technology.

paragraphs.native: Дегенмен, көбісі екеуін де ұштастыруға тырысады.
paragraphs.en: However, most try to combine both.

paragraphs.native: Мысалы, ұлттық мерекелерді әлеуметтік желіде тойлайды.
paragraphs.en: For example, they celebrate national holidays on social media.

paragraphs.native: Дәстүр мен заманауилық бірге өмір сүре алады.
paragraphs.en: Tradition and modernity can coexist.

lessons.description: Тілшінің жазушыдан алған сұхбаты туралы мәтін.

paragraphs.native: Тілші жазушыдан жаңа кітабы туралы сұрады.
paragraphs.en: The journalist asked the writer about his new book.

paragraphs.native: Жазушы бұл кітапты үш жыл жазды деді.
paragraphs.en: The writer said he wrote this book for three years.

lessons.title: Life on a Kazakh Farm
lessons.description: Айбек есімді фермердің күнделікті өмірі туралы мәтін.

readingPassage.context: Айбек есімді фермердің күнделікті өмірі туралы мәтін.

paragraphs.native: Айбек ауылда фермерлікпен айналысады.
paragraphs.en: Aibek is engaged in farming in the village.

paragraphs.native: Ол таңертең ертерек тұрады.
paragraphs.en: He gets up early in the morning.

paragraphs.native: Алдымен малын бағады, содан кейін егіске барады.
paragraphs.en: First he tends to his livestock, then goes to the field.

paragraphs.native: Жаз мезгілі — оның ең жұмысы көп кезі.

paragraphs.native: Күзде астықты жинайды.

readingPassage.context: Әлеуметтік желінің қазіргі өмірге әсері туралы мәтін.

lessons.title: The Value of Cultural Heritage
lessons.description: Мәдени мұраны сақтаудың маңыздылығы туралы мәтін.

readingPassage.context: Мәдени мұраны сақтаудың маңыздылығы туралы мәтін.

paragraphs.native: Мәдени мұра — ұлттың жады.
paragraphs.en: Cultural heritage is the memory of a nation.

paragraphs.native: Ол ескерткіштер, тіл, әдебиет және дәстүрлерден тұрады.
paragraphs.en: It consists of monuments, language, literature, and traditions.

paragraphs.native: Жаһандану кезінде кейбір дәстүрлер ұмытылып бара жатыр.
paragraphs.en: During globalization, some traditions are being forgotten.

paragraphs.native: Сондықтан жастарға мұраны үйрету маңызды.
paragraphs.en: Therefore, it is important to teach heritage to the youth.

paragraphs.native: Мектептер мен мұражайлар бұл салада үлкен рөл атқарады.
paragraphs.en: Schools and museums play a big role in this area.

lessons.title: Balancing Work and Life

readingPassage.context: Жұмыс пен өмір тепе-теңдігінің маңыздылығы туралы мәтін.

paragraphs.native: Көптеген адамдар жұмыста көп уақыт өткізеді.
paragraphs.en: Many people spend a lot of time at work.

paragraphs.native: Бұл стресс пен шаршауға әкелуі мүмкін.

paragraphs.native: Сарапшылар демалысты жоспарлауды кеңес береді.
paragraphs.en: Experts advise planning rest.

paragraphs.en: Spending time with family is also important.

paragraphs.native: Тепе-теңдік — бақытты өмірдің кілті.

lessons.title: The Rise of Renewable Energy
lessons.description: Жаңартылатын энергия көздерінің дамуы туралы мәтін.

readingPassage.context: Жаңартылатын энергия көздерінің дамуы туралы мәтін.

paragraphs.native: Соңғы жылдары жаңартылатын энергия көзіне сұраныс артты.
paragraphs.en: In recent years, demand for renewable energy sources has increased.

paragraphs.native: Күн мен жел энергиясы ең танымал көздер болып отыр.
paragraphs.en: Solar and wind energy are the most popular sources.

paragraphs.native: Қазақстанда да бірнеше жел электр станциясы салынды.
paragraphs.en: Several wind power plants have also been built in Kazakhstan.

paragraphs.native: Бұл көздер қоршаған ортаға зиян тигізбейді.
paragraphs.en: These sources don't harm the environment.

paragraphs.native: Дегенмен, бастапқы шығын әлі де жоғары.
paragraphs.en: However, the initial cost is still high.

paragraphs.native: Мамандар болашақта бағасы төмендейді деп болжайды.
paragraphs.en: Experts predict prices will decrease in the future.

exercises.question: What are the most popular renewable sources?
What has been built in Kazakhstan?
What do experts predict?

lessons.title: A Journalist's Daily Life
lessons.description: Айнұр есімді журналистің жұмысы туралы мәтін.

readingPassage.context: Айнұр есімді журналистің жұмысы туралы мәтін.

paragraphs.native: Айнұр — тәжірибелі журналист.
paragraphs.en: Ainur is an experienced journalist.

paragraphs.native: Ол күн сайын жаңалықтар жинайды.
paragraphs.en: She gathers news every day.

paragraphs.native: Кейде қиын тақырыптарды зерттеуге тура келеді.
paragraphs.en: Sometimes she has to research difficult topics.

paragraphs.native: Ол әртүрлі адамдармен сұхбат алады.
paragraphs.en: She interviews various people.

paragraphs.native: Мақаласы жарияланғанда, ол өте қуанады.
paragraphs.en: When her article is published, she is very happy.

paragraphs.native: Журналистика — жауапты да қызық мамандық.
paragraphs.en: Journalism is a responsible yet interesting profession.

exercises.question: What does Ainur do every day?
What does she do with various people?
How does she feel when her article is published?

readingPassage.context: Қазақстанның табиғи ресурстары туралы мәтін.

paragraphs.native: Қазақстан табиғи ресурстарға бай ел.
paragraphs.en: Kazakhstan is a country rich in natural resources.

paragraphs.native: Мұнай мен газ экономиканың негізгі бөлігі.
paragraphs.en: Oil and gas are a major part of the economy.

paragraphs.native: Сонымен қатар, елде түрлі кен орындары бар.
paragraphs.en: In addition, the country has various mineral deposits.

paragraphs.native: Тау-кен саласы көптеген адамға жұмыс береді.
paragraphs.en: The mining sector provides jobs for many people.

paragraphs.native: Дегенмен, экологиялық мәселелерді де ескеру керек.
paragraphs.en: However, environmental issues must also be considered.

paragraphs.native: Болашақта ресурстарды тиімді пайдалану маңызды.
paragraphs.en: Using resources efficiently in the future is important.

exercises.question: What is a major part of the economy?
What does the mining sector provide?
What must also be considered?

lessons.title: Preserving Family History
lessons.description: Қазақ халқындағы шежіре дәстүрі туралы мәтін.

readingPassage.context: Қазақ халқындағы шежіре дәстүрі туралы мәтін.

paragraphs.native: Қазақ халқында шежіре жаттауға ерекше көңіл бөлінген.
paragraphs.en: Kazakh people have paid special attention to memorizing genealogy.

paragraphs.native: Ертеде әрбір бала жеті атасын білуге тиіс болған.
paragraphs.en: In the past, every child was supposed to know their seven generations of ancestors.

paragraphs.native: Бұл дәстүр отбасылық байланысты нығайтады.
paragraphs.en: This tradition strengthens family bonds.

paragraphs.native: Қазір көптеген адам шежіресін қағазға түсіреді.
paragraphs.en: Now many people write down their genealogy on paper.

paragraphs.native: Интернет те шежіре іздеуге көмектеседі.
paragraphs.en: The internet also helps in searching for genealogy.

paragraphs.native: Өз тарихыңды білу — құндылық.
paragraphs.en: Knowing your history is a value.

exercises.question: What was every child supposed to know in the past?
What does this tradition strengthen?
What also helps in searching for genealogy now?

lessons.title: The Art Scene Today
lessons.description: Қазіргі өнер өмірінің дамуы туралы мәтін.

readingPassage.context: Қазіргі өнер өмірінің дамуы туралы мәтін.

paragraphs.native: Қазіргі өнер сахнасы жан-жақты дамып келеді.
paragraphs.en: Today's art scene is developing comprehensively.

paragraphs.native: Жас суретшілер жаңа стильдерді сынап көруде.
paragraphs.en: Young artists are experimenting with new styles.

paragraphs.native: Көрмелер қалалардың мәдени өмірін байытады.
paragraphs.en: Exhibitions enrich the cultural life of cities.

paragraphs.native: Театр мен музыка да көпшіліктің қызығушылығын тудырады.
paragraphs.en: Theater and music also arouse public interest.

paragraphs.native: Мемлекет мәдениетті қолдауға тырысады.
paragraphs.en: The state tries to support culture.

paragraphs.native: Өнер — қоғамның айнасы.
paragraphs.en: Art is the mirror of society.

exercises.question: What are young artists doing?
What do exhibitions do for cities?
What is called 'the mirror of society'?
